messages.content:
HOW YOUR CLAIM BECOMES A NUMBER (the perturbation contract):
- You never state € impacts. You emit machine-readable ops; the engine computes
  ΔTAM/ΔSAM/ΔYAM by re-running the real funnel. Any € figure you write in prose
  will be stripped.
- Ops: {nodeId, op: "set"|"scale"|"add", value} rewrite one leaf's value;
  {nodeId, op: "exclude"} removes a dimension cell from the venture's reachable
  scope ("unavailable to us" — foreclosure), which is different from shrinking it.
- Ops apply in array order. Ratio nodes must stay inside [0,1]; tamBase must stay > 0.
- Only FUNNEL-ACTIVE nodes may be perturbed (marked in the ledger you receive).
  shape.cagr and shape.cr3 are context — a demand-pace or concentration risk must
  be translated into the funnel nodes it would actually move (tamBase level,
  serviceableFactor reach, obtainableFactor pace, or a share).
- Sibling shares are NOT re-normalized when you move one share (documented v1
  simplification). If you mean a mix-SHIFT, perturb multiple siblings; if you
  mean "this slice is smaller in absolute terms", one op is correct.
- Perturbing an assumption leaf overrides whatever the user set: your claim is
  about the world, not about their slider.

Fix the hypothesis's targetNodes/perturbation so it passes validation WITHOUT changing the claim's meaning. If the claim cannot be expressed against funnel-active nodes, translate it into the funnel nodes it would actually move.

FUNNEL-ACTIVE NODES:
tamBase (EUR_M, value 300)
geo.DE (ratio, value 0.5)
geo.NL (ratio, value 0.28)
geo.PL (ratio, value 0.07)
geo.CH (ratio, value 0.08)
geo.CZ (ratio, value 0.02)
geo.AT (ratio, value 0.02)
geo.other (ratio, value 0.03)
seg.hyperscale (ratio, value 0.44)
seg.colocation (ratio, value 0.18)
seg.enterprise (ratio, value 0.29)
seg.edge (ratio, value 0.05)
seg.telecom (ratio, value 0.04)
cust.operator-large (ratio, value 0.4)
cust.operator-mid (ratio, value 0.25)
cust.oem (ratio, value 0.22)
cust.distributor (ratio, value 0.13)
serviceableFactor (ratio, value 0.55)
obtainableFactor (ratio, value 0.01)

HYPOTHESIS:
{
 "id": "poc-acceptance-reversal",
 "title": "The industrial-hardware acceptance base rate un-books a third of whatever Year-1 obtains",
 "narrative": "Whatever tiny share the venture obtains in year 1 will be obtained as pilots and first deployments — the profile's own 'conservative ramp' guarantees it. The reference class puts a number on what happens next: 30% of deployed industrial hardware systems are never fully accepted — customers sign, the vendor deploys, and six months later the gear comes back out. Intelligent PDUs are acutely exposed because acceptance hinges on post-deployment behavior: DCIM integration, metering accuracy, firmware stability under the operator's monitoring stack. The model's obtainableFactor is a gross booking rate; nothing in the funnel converts it to net-of-reversal, and for a first-year entrant the reversal doesn't just cut revenue — it destroys the reference accounts that the Year-2 3% benchmark silently assumes exist.",
 "category": "execution",
 "targetNodes": [
  "obtainableFactor"
 ],
 "mechanism": [
  {
   "step": "trigger",
   "text": "30% of deployed industrial hardware systems in the reference class were never fully accepted and required uninstallation — a measured base rate, not an anecdote",
   "refs": [
    "digest:obtainable-benchmark#c12"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "propagation",
   "text": "A pre-launch entrant's first-12-month wins are structurally pilots/POCs with acceptance clauses; intelligent-PDU acceptance depends on DCIM compatibility and metering behavior that only surfaces after deployment, in mid/enterprise accounts where the venture has no support track record",
   "refs": [
    "profile:Stage",
    "ledger:cust.operator-mid",
    "digest:cr3-vendors#c4"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "model-impact",
   "text": "obtainableFactor 0.01 is a gross capture rate; net of the acceptance-failure base rate, realized Year-1 share runs ~30% lower, and the staged benchmark's Year-2 step (3%) presumes surviving reference accounts that reversals eliminate",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:obtainableFactor",
    "digest:obtainable-benchmark#c0"
   ]
  }
 ],
 "whyMissable": "Acceptance risk hides inside a revenue number that looks conservative already — at 1% of SAM, no one audits whether the 1% stays booked. The surface appearance of prudence (a small number) conceals that the number is gross, and the funnel has no node where post-deployment reversal could even be expressed.",
 "expectedObviousness": "low",
 "falsifier": "The venture's first three CE pilot deployments passing formal customer acceptance within 90 days of install, with no rollback or return clause exercised in the following two quarters.",
 "likelihood": {
  "value": 0.35,
  "rationale": "The 30% non-acceptance figure is a single-source base rate from adjacent industrial hardware, not PDU-specific; PDUs are simpler than full industrial systems, but DCIM-integration acceptance criteria and zero support track record cut the other way.",
  "basis": "base-rate"
 },
 "perturbation": [
  {
   "nodeId": "obtainableFactor",
   "op": "scale",
   "value": 0.7,
   "note": "Gross Year-1 capture haircut by the industrial-hardware non-acceptance base rate (~30% of deployments reverse)"
  }
 ],
 "indicators": [
  {
   "signal": "Acceptance and return-rights clauses in pilot contracts",
   "where": "Redlines from CE operator procurement teams on the venture's pilot MSAs, reviewed at each signing",
   "threshold": "Acceptance windows >90 days or unconditional return rights in a majority of pilot contracts",
   "updates": "increases"
  },
  {
   "signal": "DCIM interoperability status of the product against the platforms CE operators run",
   "where": "Sunbird/Schneider EcoStruxure/Vertiv Trellis compatibility matrices and the venture's integration test reports",
   "threshold": "Product absent from ≥2 of the major DCIM compatibility lists at launch",
   "updates": "increases"
  }
 ],
 "mitigations": [
  {
   "action": "Run a structured pre-launch DCIM interoperability certification program (top-3 platforms used by CE operators) and publish the compatibility matrix before the first pilot ships",
   "type": "operational",
   "voiNodeId": null,
   "note": "Removes the single largest post-deployment acceptance failure mode before it can trigger the base rate"
  },
  {
   "action": "Instrument the first five pilots as an acceptance-rate study — formal acceptance criteria, 90-day gates, root-cause logging on any reversal — to replace the generic 30% base rate with a venture-specific one",
   "type": "information",
   "voiNodeId": "obtainableFactor",
   "note": "Converts the obtainableFactor from a benchmark guess to a measured pipeline parameter, the ledger's own stated promotion path"
  }
 ],
 "lens": "base-rate-analogy",
 "riskId": "risk.base-rate-analogy.poc-acceptance-reversal"
}

VALIDATION ERROR:
[
  {
    "code": "invalid_value",
    "values": [
      "error",
      "risk"
    ],
    "path": [
      0,
      "resolution"
    ],
    "message": "Invalid option: expected one of \"error\"|\"risk\""
  }
]

Emit the corrected hypothesis JSON (same shape).

The reference class puts a number on what happens next: 30% of deployed industrial hardware systems are never fully accepted — customers sign, the vendor deploys, and six months later the gear comes back out. Intelligent PDUs are acutely exposed because acceptance hinges on post-deployment behavior: DCIM integration, metering accuracy, firmware stability under the operator's monitoring stack.
